_README: GENERATED FROM SUPABASE - DO NOT EDIT. Supabase is the single source of truth; edit content via the admin UI. This file is regenerated on each build by scripts/export-kb-json.mjs and is not read at runtime.

meta.source: Exported from Altogether Agile knowledge_items (Supabase, is_published=true)

artifacts.name: Backlog
artifacts.oneLiner: The ordered set of items defining what the initiative will deliver, anchored to its Goal. Best held spatially as a story map (backbone of activities, slices as releases) and prioritised with an explicit scheme such as MoSCoW or WSJF; breadth changes are deliberate scope acts.
artifacts.description: The ordered set of items defining what the initiative will deliver, anchored to its Goal.
artifacts.question: What will we deliver? In what order?

components.name: Items of value
components.question: What slices of outcome are being delivered?

components.name: Priority order
components.question: How are items sequenced for delivery?

components.name: Owners
components.question: Who is accountable for each item?

components.name: Acceptance criteria
components.question: How will done be tested?

components.name: Estimates
components.question: When, given its size, is each item likely to land?

artifacts.name: Delivery Schedule
artifacts.oneLiner: The shape of delivery over time: increments now, next and later, with appetite rather than estimate where the work is uncertain. A standing wager re-bet each increment, not a promise register.
artifacts.description: The schedule and milestones of when the Backlog items will be delivered, with key releases and review points.
artifacts.question: When will each piece land?

components.name: Milestones
components.question: When are agreed points of progress expected?

components.name: Dependencies
components.question: How are items, teams, and parties linked?

components.name: Resources
components.question: Who is committed to the schedule?

components.name: Locations
components.question: Where will work take place?

components.name: Deliverables
components.question: What will arrive at each milestone?

artifacts.name: Goal
artifacts.oneLiner: The measurable goal the initiative is accountable for, anchored to the Organisation horizon intent it inherits. The root of an Impact Map and the reference every benefit is later measured against.
artifacts.description: The next meaningful objective for the initiative, cascaded from organisational Goals.
artifacts.question: Why this goal next? What outcome are we pursuing? When?

components.name: Outcome statement
components.question: What change is being aimed for in this delivery cycle?

components.name: Measure
components.question: What evidence will show progress?

components.name: Timeframe
components.question: When must the goal be met?

components.name: Owner
components.question: Who is accountable for this goal across teams?

components.name: Linkage to organisation goals
components.question: Why does this goal serve organisational intent?

artifacts.name: Governance
artifacts.oneLiner: The decision rights, forums, and thresholds within the initiative — who can decide on scope, schedule, and resourcing changes.
artifacts.description: The decision rights, forums, and thresholds within the initiative — who can decide on scope, schedule, and resourcing changes.
artifacts.question: Who decides what within this initiative, and how?

artifacts.name: Integration Standard
artifacts.oneLiner: The shared standard for what counts as ready to integrate or release across the initiative's teams.
artifacts.description: The shared standard for what counts as ready to integrate or release across the initiative's teams.
artifacts.question: How do we ensure work from different teams fits together cleanly?

artifacts.name: Outcomes
artifacts.oneLiner: The changes that would show the intent is real, each paired with a leading indicator, a target and readings over time. The evidence that returns up the cascade; if every number improved and nothing really changed, this artifact is where you would find out.
artifacts.description: The measurable evidence that tests whether the Goal is being met, drawn from real use of what the initiative has produced.
artifacts.question: Is the initiative producing the outcomes we hoped for?

components.name: Outcomes observed
components.question: What change did coordination actually deliver?

components.name: Beneficiary feedback
components.question: Who used the result, and what do they say?

components.name: Comparison to delivery goals
components.question: Why did the outcome differ from the goal?

components.name: Review point
components.question: When in the delivery cycle is this review made?

components.name: Method
components.question: How were evidence and feedback collected?

artifacts.name: Purpose
artifacts.oneLiner: Why this initiative exists and what it is for, in service of the host organisation's Purpose. For a project or programme, this is the reason the temporary organisation has been formed. For a product or service, this is the reason the product exists and the value it is built to create.
artifacts.description: Why this initiative exists and what it is for, in service of the host organisation's Purpose. For a project or programme, this is the reason the temporary organisation has been formed. For a product or service, this is the reason the product exists and the value it is built to create.
artifacts.question: Why does this initiative exist?

artifacts.name: Solution Design
artifacts.oneLiner: The shape of the solution held as the current decision trail plus snapshots, never a finished blueprint. Options stay plural until probes decide; the design is the set of decisions the team currently stands behind.
artifacts.description: The articulated description of what the solution looks like — its components, interfaces, behaviour, data — at a level that lets multiple teams build coherent parts.
artifacts.question: What will the solution look like? What will it do?

artifacts.name: Stakeholder Map
artifacts.oneLiner: Who surrounds the initiative and how decisions travel: the onion of those who deliver, are consulted and are affected. Governance follows these people, not the calendar.
artifacts.description: The stakeholders with power to fund, block, support, or override the initiative, and what each one needs from it.
artifacts.question: Who can stop this, regardless of merit? Who needs convincing?

artifacts.name: Team Topology
artifacts.oneLiner: The set of teams delivering the initiative, the shape of each, and the way they interact.
artifacts.description: The set of teams delivering the initiative, the shape of each, and the way they interact.
artifacts.question: Who builds what? How do the teams relate?

artifacts.name: User and Beneficiary Profile
artifacts.oneLiner: Who the initiative serves, detailed from their own side: personas derived from the goal's actors, with jobs to be done, pains and behaviours. Choosing which profiles to design for is a breadth-of-scope decision; journey maps hang off these profiles.
artifacts.description: The articulated understanding of who the initiative serves and what they need — their goals, contexts, jobs, and the value they would receive.
artifacts.question: Who are we serving, and what do they need?

artifacts.name: Vision
artifacts.oneLiner: The change this initiative exists to make, stated as someone's world before and after. The founding hypothesis every other Coordination artifact serves; best generated with a Product Vision Board or a Before-and-After narrative.
artifacts.description: The future state the initiative is bringing about — what the world looks like when the work is done.
artifacts.question: Where is this initiative taking us?

components.name: Aspirational outcome
components.question: Why is this coordinated effort being undertaken?

components.name: Beneficiaries
components.question: Who is this effort for?

components.name: Scope of change
components.question: What will be affected by the effort?

components.name: Boundaries
components.question: Where does the effort start and stop?

components.name: Timeframe
components.question: When does the vision apply?

artifacts.name: Way of Working
artifacts.oneLiner: How the initiative's people agree to work: working agreements plus the improvement actions retrospectives produce. Refreshed when the people or the work change; the standing challenge is what works well that the team has quietly become afraid to change.
artifacts.description: The shared delivery practices, engineering standards, and ceremonies across the initiative's teams.
artifacts.question: How do the teams in this initiative work together?

components.name: Participating teams
components.question: Who is bound by the charter?

components.name: Cadences and events
components.question: When do teams meet, review, and integrate?

components.name: Communication channels
components.question: Where do conversations happen and decisions get recorded?

components.name: Decision rights
components.question: How are decisions allocated across roles?

components.name: Working agreements
components.question: How will teams work together day to day?

components.name: Escalation paths
components.question: How are issues escalated when local decision rights are exceeded?

artifacts.name: Brand
artifacts.oneLiner: How the organisation is recognised, remembered, and trusted. The visible expression of the Value Proposition.
artifacts.description: How the organisation is recognised, remembered, and trusted. The visible expression of the Value Proposition.
artifacts.question: Who are we in the customer's mind?

artifacts.name: Business Model
artifacts.oneLiner: How the organisation creates, delivers, and captures value. The outward-facing commercial logic.
artifacts.description: How the organisation creates, delivers, and captures value. The outward-facing commercial logic.
artifacts.question: What do we sell, to whom, and how do we make money?

components.name: Customer segments
components.question: Who does the organisation serve?

components.name: Value proposition
components.question: What does the organisation offer each segment?

components.name: Channels
components.question: Where does value reach the customer?

components.name: Customer relationships
components.question: How does the organisation engage with each segment?

components.name: Revenue streams
components.question: How is value captured commercially?

components.name: Key activities
components.question: How does the organisation create and deliver value?

components.name: Key resources
components.question: What assets does the model depend on?

components.name: Key partners
components.question: Who outside the organisation does the model rely on?

components.name: Cost structure
components.question: How is the economic shape of the model put together?

artifacts.name: Goals
artifacts.oneLiner: Time-bound, measurable commitments on the way to the Vision.
artifacts.description: Time-bound, measurable commitments on the way to the Vision.
artifacts.question: What will we achieve, by when?

components.name: Outcome statement
components.question: What change is this goal seeking to bring about?

components.name: Measure of success
components.question: What evidence will show the goal has been achieved?

components.name: Timeframe
components.question: When must this goal be achieved?

components.name: Owner
components.question: Who is accountable for achieving this goal?

components.name: Strategic linkage
components.question: Why is this goal a priority now, and how does it serve Vision and Strategy?

artifacts.name: Governance
artifacts.oneLiner: The decision rights, forums, and thresholds that determine who decides what across the organisation.
artifacts.description: The decision rights, forums, and thresholds that determine who decides what across the organisation.
artifacts.question: Who decides what, and how are decisions made?

artifacts.name: Mission
artifacts.oneLiner: What the organisation does about its Purpose, in the present tense.
artifacts.description: What the organisation does about its Purpose, in the present tense.
artifacts.question: Why are we here today, and what do we do about it?

components.name: Activity
components.question: What does the organisation actually do?

components.name: Beneficiary
components.question: Who does the organisation serve through that activity?

components.name: Distinction
components.question: Why is the contribution different from others doing similar work?

components.name: Operating arena
components.question: Where does the organisation operate?

artifacts.name: Operating Model
artifacts.oneLiner: The capabilities, processes, structures, technology, partners, and people that turn the Business Model into a working reality.
artifacts.description: The capabilities, processes, structures, technology, partners, and people that turn the Business Model into a working reality.
artifacts.question: How do we run, day to day?

components.name: Structure
components.question: Where does authority and grouping sit in the organisation?

components.name: Roles and accountabilities
components.question: Who is responsible for what?

components.name: Processes
components.question: How does work get done repeatably?

components.name: Information flows
components.question: How do data, decisions, and signals move through the organisation?

components.name: Governance
components.question: Who decides what, and in which forum?

components.name: Locations and footprint
components.question: Where does work physically and digitally take place?

artifacts.name: Portfolio Backlog
artifacts.oneLiner: The ordered set of strategic initiatives the organisation will pursue to meet its Goals.
artifacts.description: The ordered set of strategic initiatives the organisation will pursue to meet its Goals.
artifacts.question: What initiatives will we run? In what order?

artifacts.name: Portfolio Roadmap
artifacts.oneLiner: The schedule and milestones across the Portfolio Backlog — when initiatives are sequenced for delivery, with key review and decision points.
artifacts.description: The schedule and milestones across the Portfolio Backlog — when initiatives are sequenced for delivery, with key review and decision points.
artifacts.question: When will each initiative deliver, and how do they sequence?

artifacts.name: Purpose
artifacts.oneLiner: The timeless reason the organisation exists.
artifacts.description: The timeless reason the organisation exists.
artifacts.question: Why do we exist at all?

components.name: Reason for being
components.question: Why does the organisation exist beyond making money?

components.name: Beneficiaries
components.question: Who are the people whose lives the organisation aims to improve?

components.name: Distinctive contribution
components.question: What unique change does the organisation seek to make in the world?

components.name: Enduring horizon
components.question: When, if ever, should this Purpose be expected to change?

artifacts.name: Strategic Outcomes and Market Signals
artifacts.oneLiner: The evidence that tests whether the Strategy and Goals are working: customer behaviour, financial performance, market shifts, regulatory changes.
artifacts.description: The evidence that tests whether the Strategy and Goals are working: customer behaviour, financial performance, market shifts, regulatory changes.
artifacts.question: Is the Strategy actually delivering, and what is the world telling us?

components.name: Outcomes observed
components.question: What change has actually happened?

components.name: Stakeholder feedback
components.question: Who has been affected, and what do they say about the result?

components.name: Comparison to intent
components.question: Why does the outcome differ from what was intended?

components.name: Review point
components.question: When is this review being made?

components.name: Method of evidence-gathering
components.question: How was the evidence collected?

artifacts.name: Strategy
artifacts.oneLiner: The choices about where to compete and how to win.
artifacts.description: The choices about where to compete and how to win.
artifacts.question: Where will we play? How will we win?

components.name: Winning aspiration
components.question: Why is the organisation pursuing this strategy?

components.name: Where to play
components.question: Where will the organisation compete?

components.name: How to win
components.question: How will the organisation earn advantage in those arenas?

components.name: Capabilities required
components.question: What strengths must the organisation have to execute the strategy?

components.name: Management systems
components.question: How will the strategy be governed and operated?

artifacts.name: Value Proposition
artifacts.oneLiner: The promise made to the customer; the hinge of the Business Model.
artifacts.description: The promise made to the customer; the hinge of the Business Model.
artifacts.question: Why would they choose us?

components.name: Beneficiary segment
components.question: Who is the proposition for?

components.name: Jobs to be done
components.question: What is the beneficiary trying to achieve?

components.name: Pain relievers
components.question: Why does this proposition matter against the beneficiary’s pains?

components.name: Gain creators
components.question: Why does this proposition matter against the beneficiary’s gains?

components.name: Differentiation
components.question: How is this proposition different from the alternatives?

artifacts.name: Values
artifacts.oneLiner: Behaviours the organisation refuses to trade away, even when it would be profitable to.
artifacts.description: Behaviours the organisation refuses to trade away, even when it would be profitable to.
artifacts.question: How do we behave on the way?

components.name: Named behaviours
components.question: How do people in this organisation behave when at their best?

components.name: Identity markers
components.question: Who belongs in this organisation, and what marks them out?

components.name: Decision filters
components.question: Why might one course of action be preferred over another?

components.name: Continuity commitment
components.question: When are these values held, and when might they be tested?

artifacts.name: Vision
artifacts.oneLiner: The future the organisation is working toward, set far enough out to be ambitious and close enough to be accountable.
artifacts.description: The future the organisation is working toward, set far enough out to be ambitious and close enough to be accountable.
artifacts.question: Where are we going? When will we get there?

components.name: Aspirational future state
components.question: Why is this future worth striving for?

components.name: Time horizon
components.question: When does the organisation aim to reach this future state?

components.name: Success picture
components.question: What does success look like when the vision is achieved?

components.name: Scope of ambition
components.question: Where, in which arenas or markets, does this vision apply?

artifacts.name: Delivery Health Metrics
artifacts.oneLiner: The leading indicators of how the team's delivery system is performing.
artifacts.description: The leading indicators of how the team's delivery system is performing.
artifacts.question: How fast? How predictable? How clean?

artifacts.name: Goal
artifacts.oneLiner: The intent for the team's current cycle, cascaded from the initiative Goal.
artifacts.description: The intent for the team's current cycle, cascaded from the initiative Goal.
artifacts.question: Why this cycle? What must be true by the end of it?

components.name: Outcome
components.question: What is the team working towards this iteration?

components.name: Success measure
components.question: What evidence will show the goal is met?

components.name: Timeframe
components.question: When does this goal apply?

components.name: Owning team
components.question: Who owns this goal?

components.name: Reason
components.question: Why does this goal matter now?

artifacts.name: Output
artifacts.oneLiner: The integrated, usable result of the cycle that meets the Quality Bar.
artifacts.description: The integrated, usable result of the cycle that meets the Quality Bar.
artifacts.question: What did the cycle actually produce?

components.name: Working output
components.question: What did the team produce?

components.name: Definition of done met
components.question: How was the increment confirmed as done?

components.name: Recipients
components.question: Who receives or uses the increment?

components.name: Date completed
components.question: When was the increment made available?

components.name: Environment
components.question: Where is the increment deployed or in use?

artifacts.name: Output Feedback
artifacts.oneLiner: The evidence from real use of the Output — whether anyone uses it, and whether it produces the outcomes the initiative Goal expected.
artifacts.description: The evidence from real use of the Output — whether anyone uses it, and whether it produces the outcomes the initiative Goal expected.
artifacts.question: Is what we built being used? Is it producing the change we hoped for?

components.name: Outcomes observed
components.question: What change did the team’s work produce?

components.name: User feedback
components.question: Who used the result, and what do they say?

components.name: Comparison to team goal
components.question: Why did the outcome differ from the team goal?

components.name: Review point
components.question: When in the iteration is this review made?

components.name: Method
components.question: How was the evidence gathered?

artifacts.name: Plan
artifacts.oneLiner: The team's own plan for delivering the Goal during the cycle — the schedule and sequence of the chosen work.
artifacts.description: The team's own plan for delivering the Goal during the cycle — the schedule and sequence of the chosen work.
artifacts.question: When will each item move? In what order?

components.name: Iteration scope
components.question: What has the team committed to in this iteration?

components.name: Iteration goal
components.question: Why does this iteration matter?

components.name: Capacity
components.question: Who is available, and for how much effort?

components.name: Schedule of events
components.question: When do the iteration’s touch-points happen?

components.name: Dependencies
components.question: How is the plan linked to other teams or work?

artifacts.name: Quality Bar
artifacts.oneLiner: The shared standard every team output must meet to be considered complete.
artifacts.description: The shared standard every team output must meet to be considered complete.
artifacts.question: How do we know the work is really finished?

artifacts.name: Selected Work Items
artifacts.oneLiner: The items pulled from the initiative Backlog into the team's current cycle.
artifacts.description: The items pulled from the initiative Backlog into the team's current cycle.
artifacts.question: What have we taken on this cycle?

components.name: Items
components.question: What pieces of work are waiting?

components.name: Priority
components.question: How are items ordered for the team to pull?

components.name: Owner
components.question: Who grooms and orders the backlog?

components.name: Acceptance criteria
components.question: How will each item be confirmed as done?

components.name: Estimates
components.question: When, given its size, is each item likely to land?

artifacts.name: Working Agreement
artifacts.oneLiner: The behavioural and practice norms a team chooses to operate by.
artifacts.description: The behavioural and practice norms a team chooses to operate by.
artifacts.question: How do we choose to behave as a team?

components.name: Team members
components.question: Who is in the team?

components.name: Purpose
components.question: Why does this team exist?

components.name: Goals
components.question: What does success look like for this team?

components.name: Working agreements
components.question: How will the team work together?

components.name: Boundaries and scope
components.question: Where does the team’s remit start and stop?

components.name: Cadences
components.question: When does the team meet, review, and plan?

techniques.name: 5 Whys
techniques.description: A simple root cause analysis method that uncovers underlying problems by asking 'why' multiple times.

techniques.name: A/B Testing
techniques.description: A technique to compare multiple variants (A vs B) in a controlled experiment to identify the more effective design or feature based on measurable user behavior.

techniques.name: Acceptance Criteria
techniques.description: Specific, measurable conditions that a solution must meet to be accepted by users or stakeholders. Often written in Given-When-Then format.

techniques.name: Accessibility Audit
techniques.description: Technique to evaluate digital accessibility compliance and inclusion standards (e.g., WCAG).

techniques.name: Affinity Mapping
techniques.description: A sense-making technique for organising a large number of ideas, observations or research notes into natural groupings. Participants cluster related items, then name each cluster, revealing themes that were not obvious in the raw data.

techniques.name: Architectural Runway and Enablers
techniques.description: Building just-enough technical foundation ahead of features so future work can flow without big up-front design.

techniques.name: Assumption Mapping
techniques.description: A method to surface, categorize, and prioritize assumptions underpinning product ideas or business models.

techniques.name: Backlog Refinement
techniques.description: Ongoing clarifying, splitting and ordering of upcoming backlog items so they are ready before a team commits to them.

techniques.name: Big Room / PI Planning
techniques.description: A cross-team planning event where all teams plan a shared increment together and surface dependencies face to face.

techniques.name: Build-Measure-Learn
techniques.description: The core loop: build an experiment, measure real behaviour, and learn whether to persevere or pivot.

techniques.name: Burndown / Burnup Chart
techniques.description: A simple chart tracking remaining or completed work against time to expose whether a goal is on track.

techniques.name: Business Case
techniques.description: The justification for investment and its continued justification in flight: vision, options with a chosen one, costs and benefits as ranges, investment appraisal and risk. Outline at Feasibility, baselined at the commit point, re-checked each increment: kill or continue.

techniques.name: Business Model Canvas
techniques.description: A strategic management framework, created by Alexander Osterwalder (2005 thesis, refined with Yves Pigneur), popularized through the book Business Model Generation (2010). Widely used as a global standard for business model design and innovation.

techniques.name: Classes of Service
techniques.description: Distinct handling policies for different work types (expedite, fixed-date, standard) to manage urgency and risk.

techniques.name: Cohort Analysis
techniques.description: Tracking groups of users over time to read actionable metrics rather than vanity totals.

techniques.name: Communities of Practice
techniques.description: Cross-team groups that share and align practice in a discipline without central control.

techniques.name: Concierge / Wizard of Oz Test
techniques.description: Manually delivering a service behind the scenes to test demand before building the technology.

techniques.name: Contextual Inquiry
techniques.description: A field research method involving observation and semi-structured interviews while users perform real tasks in their context.

techniques.name: Cost of Delay
techniques.description: Donald Reinertsen's economic lens: what it costs per unit time not to have a thing. The number that turns prioritisation arguments into arithmetic and underpins WSJF.

techniques.name: Cumulative Flow Diagram
techniques.description: A visual chart showing how work is progressing through stages, used to spot bottlenecks and flow issues.

techniques.name: Customer Interviews
techniques.description: Direct, structured or semi-structured conversations with users to explore their needs, motivations, and pain points.

techniques.name: Daily Standup
techniques.description: A short daily sync where the team inspects progress toward the goal and surfaces impediments early.

techniques.name: Definition of Done (DoD)
techniques.description: Scrum's quality bar (Schwaber and Sutherland): the agreed conditions under which work is genuinely complete, including testing and review. The convergent half of a development approach; tightens at retrospectives as the team learns what bites.

techniques.name: Delivery Plan
techniques.description: An agile delivery plan is an adaptive plan for a product or project delivery.  The plan does not go into the detail of a delivery tkmeboxes (sprints), rather it is a high-level schedule of a delivery.

techniques.name: Dependency Mapping / Program Board
techniques.description: Visualising cross-team dependencies and their timing so they can be sequenced or removed.

techniques.name: Design Studio Workshop
techniques.description: A collaborative sketching method for exploring and critiquing UI or solution ideas across team members.

techniques.name: Dot Voting
techniques.description: A simple group decision technique where participants allocate a fixed number of dots across options to reveal collective preference. It quickly narrows a long list to a shortlist without lengthy debate.

techniques.name: Empathy Map
techniques.description: A fast collaborative read of what a beneficiary says, thinks, does and feels. Lighter than a persona; often the bridge from stakeholder talk to genuine needs.

techniques.name: Estimation (Story Points, T-shirt sizing)
techniques.description: Relative estimation techniques that measure effort or complexity rather than time. Useful for forecasting and iteration planning.

techniques.name: Example Mapping
techniques.description: A collaborative discovery technique that breaks a user story into its business rules and concrete examples. Using four colours of card (story, rules, examples, questions), the team explores a story together before development, surfacing unknowns early and agreeing scope.

techniques.name: Experiment Cards
techniques.description: Test and Learning Cards after David Bland and Alexander Osterwalder (Strategyzer): each probe made falsifiable as hypothesis, test, metric and learning. The safe-to-fail instrument that selects between solution options before commitment.
techniques.source: Testing Business Ideas (David J. Bland & Alexander Osterwalder, 2019)

techniques.name: Explicit Workflow Policies
techniques.description: Making the rules for how work moves through each state visible and agreed, including a clear definition of workflow.

techniques.name: Facilitated Workshop
techniques.description: A structured, neutrally facilitated session to reach shared decisions quickly across stakeholders.

techniques.name: Fake Door Tests
techniques.description: A lightweight experiment that simulates a feature or offer to test user interest before investing in development.

techniques.name: Feature Slicing
techniques.description: Breaking down large features into smaller, independently valuable slices that can be developed and released incrementally.

techniques.name: Feature Toggles
techniques.description: A technique that allows features to be deployed but hidden or selectively enabled via toggles for safer delivery.

techniques.name: Fishbone Diagram
techniques.description: Also known as a Cause-and-Effect Diagram or Ishikawa Diagram, it helps teams visualize possible causes of a problem across categories like people, process, and tools.

techniques.name: Flow Metrics
techniques.description: Metrics like Cycle Time, Lead Time, and Throughput used to evaluate and improve delivery performance.

techniques.name: Functional Decomposition
techniques.description: Breaking down complex processes or systems into smaller, more manageable parts for analysis, estimation, or development planning.

techniques.name: Gherkin/BDD
techniques.description: A collaborative approach using structured 'Given-When-Then' syntax to define system behavior and automate acceptance testing.

techniques.name: Happiness Metric / Niko-Niko
techniques.description: A visual or quantitative method of checking team morale regularly, helping spot burnout or disengagement early.

techniques.name: Heuristic Evaluation
techniques.description: Experts review a user interface against recognized usability principles (heuristics) to find friction points and opportunities for improvement.

techniques.name: How Might We?
techniques.description: IDEO's reframing move: turn a pain or insight into an open design question. The divergent opener for needs work before any solution is named.

techniques.name: Impact Mapping
techniques.description: Gojko Adzic's four-level tree from Why (goal) to Who (actors) to How (behaviour changes) to What (deliverables). Starts from a business goal, not a system, so non-technical groups take to it quickly; the map is disposable and re-walked at each increment review so goals that no longer connect to deliverables get pruned.

techniques.name: Impact vs Effort Matrix
techniques.description: A grid used to quickly assess and group items by their estimated impact and effort, guiding prioritization.

techniques.name: Impediment Lists
techniques.description: A running list of blockers or issues that hinder team progress, owned by the Scrum Master or team lead for resolution.

techniques.name: Inspect and Adapt
techniques.description: A recurring cross-team retrospective and problem-solving workshop on the whole system of delivery.

techniques.name: Investment Appraisal
techniques.description: Maps development and ongoing running costs against benefits over time, answering when the costs land against the benefits and whether the initiative can survive the gap.

techniques.name: Iterative Development and Modelling
techniques.description: Evolving the solution in short iterations with frequent demonstration rather than a single big build.

techniques.name: Jobs To Be Done
techniques.description: Clayton Christensen's framing: people hire products to get a job done. Asks what progress the person is trying to make in a circumstance, which surfaces needs that feature-talk hides; pairs with personas to explain why a behaviour change would actually happen.

techniques.name: Journey Mapping
techniques.description: Service design practice, popularised by Adaptive Path: one persona's experience as stages across the top with doing, thinking, feeling and pain rows beneath. Exposes where asserted impacts meet reality; its stages seed a story map backbone and its pains become impact or story candidates.

techniques.name: Lean Canvas
techniques.description: Ash Maurya's adaptation of Osterwalder's Business Model Canvas: an investable case on one page with its assumptions exposed for testing. The natural outline Business Case form at Feasibility, carrying best, expected and worst ranges.

techniques.name: Lean Governance Tools
techniques.description: Lightweight tools or checklists to ensure delivery aligns with strategic goals, risks are surfaced, and compliance is addressed without bureaucracy.
techniques.source: Lean, Agile (Scaled Agile Framework), BCS (Project Delivery), AgilePM

techniques.name: Mind Mapping
techniques.description: A visual brainstorming technique that captures related ideas, problems, or solutions in a branching tree format.

techniques.name: Minimum Viable Product (MVP)
techniques.description: The smallest thing you can build to test a critical assumption and start learning from real users.

techniques.name: MoSCoW Prioritization
techniques.description: Dai Clegg's prioritisation, originating within DSDM: Must, Should, Could, Won't (this time). Forces a genuine Must apart from the merely wanted and makes exclusion explicit; native vocabulary for AgilePM-aligned work.

techniques.name: Multi-team Backlog Refinement
techniques.description: Several teams refining one shared product backlog together to keep a single ordering.

techniques.name: OKRs (Objectives and Key Results)
techniques.description: Andy Grove's Objectives and Key Results, popularised by John Doerr: ties intended outcomes to measurable signals the organisation already tracks. The natural formalisation of goals and the review form for benefits.

techniques.name: Opportunity Canvas
techniques.description: A lightweight canvas to identify customer problems, metrics, and potential solutions in early product discovery.

techniques.name: Opportunity Solution Tree
techniques.description: A visual tool that links desired outcomes to opportunities and solution experiments for continuous discovery.

techniques.name: Personas
techniques.description: Evidence-based archetypes in the Alan Cooper tradition: a named character with context, goals, pains and behaviours. The actor names who matters from the goal's side; the persona details them from their own side, and choosing which personas to design for is a breadth-of-scope decision.
techniques.source: Agile (Discovery & Framing), Agile (User-Centered Analysis), BCS (Analytical Techniques)

techniques.name: PESTLE
techniques.description: A technique used to analyze external macro-environmental factors—Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Legal, and Environmental—that could impact the strategy.

techniques.name: Pivot or Persevere
techniques.description: A scheduled decision, made on evidence, to change direction or keep going on the current hypothesis.

techniques.name: Planning Poker
techniques.description: A consensus-based estimation technique where team members assign story points to work items using cards, helping uncover uncertainty or assumptions.

techniques.name: Portfolio Kanban
techniques.description: Visualising and limiting big initiatives (epics) as they flow through funnel-to-done states at portfolio level.

techniques.name: Portfolio Roadmaps
techniques.description: High-level visual timelines showing planned delivery of major initiatives and their alignment to strategic themes across the organization.

techniques.name: Prioritised Requirements List
techniques.description: A MoSCoW-prioritised list of requirements that fixes time and cost while flexing scope.

techniques.name: Problem Statement
techniques.description: A concise framing of the core user/customer problem a product or feature is intended to solve.
techniques.source: Design Thinking / Engineering Problem Solving (20th century)

techniques.name: Product Backlog
techniques.description: An ordered list of all the things known to be needed to add value to a product

techniques.name: Product Vision Board
techniques.description: Roman Pichler's one-page vision: Vision, Target Group, Needs, Product, Business Goals. States driver and objective in a form people can repeat from memory; the founding hypothesis a new product journey starts from.

techniques.name: Prototyping
techniques.description: Building low-fidelity, often throwaway models of a solution to test understanding and reduce risk early.

techniques.name: Release Planning
techniques.description: Determining which features or capabilities will be delivered in upcoming releases, balancing value, risk, and capacity across time.

techniques.name: Replenishment Meeting
techniques.description: A regular cadence to pull the next most valuable items into the committed queue.

techniques.name: Retrospective Techniques
techniques.description: After Norman Kerth and Derby and Larsen: the regular heartbeat that turns delivery into learning. What worked, what would most improve us, one action; the mechanism that keeps the ways-of-working artifact alive.

techniques.name: RICE
techniques.description: A quantitative prioritization method that scores initiatives using Reach, Impact, Confidence, and Effort to inform sequencing.

techniques.name: Risk Analysis
techniques.description: Identifying and evaluating risks related to release scope, timing, or quality, and determining mitigation or acceptance strategies.

techniques.name: Rolling Wave Planning
techniques.description: A progressive elaboration technique where near-term work is planned in detail, while future work is planned at a higher level.

techniques.name: Scrum of Scrums
techniques.description: A regular cross-team sync where representatives coordinate shared work and resolve inter-team impediments.

techniques.name: Service Blueprint
techniques.description: A diagram that maps out the user‚Äö√¥s interaction with the service and the behind-the-scenes processes, systems, and people enabling those experiences.

techniques.name: Service Level Expectation (SLE)
techniques.description: A probabilistic forecast of how long an item will take to flow, used as a delivery commitment.

techniques.name: Smoke / Landing Page Test
techniques.description: A lightweight test, such as a landing page, that measures intent to buy or sign up before building.

techniques.name: Spikes
techniques.description: Short, time-boxed tasks aimed at answering questions or exploring approaches to reduce technical, UX, or business uncertainty.

techniques.name: Sprint Review / Demo
techniques.description: A working-software review with stakeholders to inspect the increment and adapt the backlog from real feedback.

techniques.name: Story Mapping
techniques.description: Jeff Patton's spatial backlog: a backbone of user activities across the top, options and detail hanging beneath, sliced horizontally into releases. The map is the prioritised requirements list in spatial form; re-slicing it re-prioritises the list and keeps breadth-of-scope decisions visible.

techniques.name: Story Splitting
techniques.description: Breaking down large or complex user stories into smaller, testable pieces that can be delivered within a sprint.

techniques.name: Storyboards
techniques.description: A sequential set of sketches or images to show how a user interacts with a product or service over time.

techniques.name: SWOT
techniques.description: A structured planning method to identify internal Strengths and Weaknesses, and external Opportunities and Threats affecting an initiative or organization.

techniques.name: System Demo
techniques.description: An integrated demo of the whole solution across teams to get aligned feedback each increment.

techniques.name: Task Boards
techniques.description: Visual boards (physical or digital) that track the status of tasks from """To Do""" to """Done""". Common in Kanban and Scrum.

techniques.name: Team Working Agreements
techniques.description: Team-developed rules and expectations for how they will work together, communicate, and resolve conflict.

techniques.name: Technical Debt Radar
techniques.description: A tool or method to identify, categorize, and visualize technical debt, enabling better discussions and decision-making about system health.
techniques.source: Ward Cunningham (1992, metaphor); ThoughtWorks Technology Radar (2000s)

techniques.name: Throughput / Monte Carlo Forecasting
techniques.description: Forecasting delivery dates probabilistically from historical throughput rather than from estimates.

techniques.name: Timeboxing
techniques.description: Fixing a hard time limit for a piece of work so that scope and decisions flex to fit the box, rather than the deadline moving.

techniques.name: Usability Testing
techniques.description: A technique to evaluate how easily users can complete tasks using a prototype or working system, often revealing UX issues.
techniques.source: Jakob Nielsen & Rolf Molich (1990s, UX Practice)

techniques.name: Use Cases
techniques.description: A structured textual technique to describe system interactions from the user's point of view, detailing steps and exceptions for each interaction.

techniques.name: User Journey Analytics
techniques.description: A method for analyzing quantitative journey data alongside qualitative insights to identify drop-offs, pain points, and optimization areas.

techniques.name: User Stories
techniques.description: Requirements as conversations in a beneficiary's voice: who, what, why, with acceptance criteria. Written against INVEST; each story should trace to the deliverable and persona it serves.

techniques.name: Validated Learning / Innovation Accounting
techniques.description: Measuring progress by evidence of learning about the business model, not by features shipped.

techniques.name: Value Proposition Canvas
techniques.description: A visual tool used to ensure a product or service is positioned around what the customer values and needs. It maps customer jobs, pains, and gains to product features.

techniques.name: Velocity Forecasting
techniques.description: Using a team"""s historical delivery rate (velocity) to estimate how much work can be completed in a future release cycle.

techniques.name: WIP Limits
techniques.description: Capping the number of items allowed in each workflow state to expose bottlenecks and improve flow.

techniques.name: Working Agreements
techniques.description: Common XP and Scrum practice: governance as a one-page social contract the team wrote itself. Refreshed when the people or the work change, not on a schedule.

techniques.name: WSJF (Weighted Shortest Job First)
techniques.description: Cost of Delay over job size, after Donald Reinertsen and popularised in SAFe: business value, time criticality, and risk reduction or opportunity enablement scored against duration. Orders work by economic urgency rather than gut feel.
